title: Centinodium aviculare
taxonomy: Centinodium aviculare is a species of flowering plant in the family Asteraceae. This plant is native to the Pacific Islands. Further classification of Centinodium aviculare would involve its genus and species level classification, which places it in the order Asterales. The plant's classification is further defined by its kingdom, being classified under the kingdom Plantae. It is a part of the phylum Magnoliophyta and division Magnoliophyta as well.
uses: Centinodium aviculare, also known as the 'tree daisy' or 'white cloud', has been found to have various medicinal properties. It is used in traditional medicine to treat various ailments including fever, colds, and coughs. The plant is believed to possess anti-inflammatory properties, which may help in reducing swelling and pain. Its leaves are also used to treat skin conditions such as acne and eczema. However, further research is needed to confirm its full range of medicinal uses.

properties: Centinodium aviculare, also known as the beach hibiscus, has been traditionally used in some cultures for its therapeutic properties. parts: Centinodium aviculare is a shrub native to Australia, and its medicinal properties have been documented in traditional indigenous medicine. The roots of the Centinodium aviculare plant are often used to create a treatment for various skin conditions and wounds. In addition to the roots, the leaves and bark of the plant are also utilized for their medicinal properties. The plant's extracts have been used to treat conditions such as eczema, acne, and other skin irritations. The uses of Centinodium aviculare have been passed down through generations of indigenous Australians for its potential health benefits.
intro: Centinodium aviculare, also known as Centinodia or New Zealand peppermint, is a shrub-like plant native to New Zealand and Australia, belonging to the family Verbenaceae. The plant has been traditionally used for its medicinal properties, offering benefits such as relief from digestive issues, inflammation, and respiratory problems due to its anti-inflammatory and antiseptic properties. The bioactive constituents of Centinodium aviculare include flavonoids, phenolic acids, and essential oils, which contribute to its therapeutic effects. In herbal medicine, Centinodium aviculare is commonly prepared as a tea, infusion, or tincture, often used to alleviate symptoms associated with colds, fever, and digestive discomfort.
